copyright_note: All resources are original Quanta Aptus content. No Cambridge source text included.

resources.title: Calculation Drill — Motion, forces and energy
resources.student_prompt: A small delivery robot travels at a steady speed of 1.8 m/s for 75 s. Calculate the distance travelled.
resources.worked_solution: Use distance = speed × time. distance = 1.8 × 75 = 135 m. The robot travels 135 m.
resources.marking_guidance: 1 mark for selecting distance = speed × time; 1 mark for correct substitution; 1 mark for 135 m with unit.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Some students multiply by 75 minutes instead of 75 seconds. Check the unit of time before calculating."]
resources.teacher_notes: Good first item for building the formula triangle: d = vt.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Worked Example — Motion, forces and energy
resources.student_prompt: Worked example: A drone's horizontal speed increases uniformly from 2.0 m/s to 14 m/s in 6.0 s, then remains at 14 m/s for 9.0 s. Calculate the horizontal distance travelled.
resources.worked_solution: Step 1: During uniform acceleration, use average speed = (initial speed + final speed)/2 = (2.0 + 14)/2 = 8.0 m/s. Step 2: Distance during acceleration = 8.0 × 6.0 = 48 m. Step 3: Distance at constant speed = 14 × 9.0 = 126 m. Step 4: Total distance = 48 + 126 = 174 m.
resources.marking_guidance: 1 mark for average speed; 1 mark for acceleration distance; 1 mark for constant-speed distance; 1 mark for total distance and unit.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students often use the final speed for the whole acceleration interval. Uniform acceleration requires average speed or triangular/trapezium graph area."]
resources.teacher_notes: Useful for demonstrating that graph-area and average-speed methods are equivalent.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Calculation Drill — Motion, forces and energy
resources.student_prompt: A 1.5 kW heater warms water in a container. It transfers 270 kJ of useful thermal energy. Calculate the time in minutes if all the heater energy is useful.
resources.worked_solution: Convert power: 1.5 kW = 1500 W. Convert energy: 270 kJ = 270000 J. Use t = E/P. t = 270000 ÷ 1500 = 180 s = 3.0 min.
resources.marking_guidance: 1 mark for unit conversions; 1 mark for rearranging t = E/P; 1 mark for 180 s; 1 mark for 3.0 min.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["A common error is to divide power by energy. Check that time should become seconds."]
resources.teacher_notes: Require students to show both seconds and minutes to reveal unit confidence.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Short Answer Calculation — Motion, forces and energy
resources.student_prompt: A 2.0 kg sample is warmed by 12 °C using 60000 J of energy. Calculate the specific heat capacity of the sample.
resources.marking_guidance: 1 mark for rearrangement; 1 mark for denominator; 1 mark for final answer with unit.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["The denominator is mass times temperature change, not mass plus temperature change."]
resources.teacher_notes: Useful for practising rearrangement of three-factor equations.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Worked Example — Motion, forces and energy
resources.student_prompt: Worked example: A 1.1 kW electric warmer runs for 5.0 minutes. Calculate the energy transferred in joules.
resources.worked_solution: Step 1: Convert power to watts: 1.1 kW = 1100 W. Step 2: Convert time to seconds: 5.0 min = 300 s. Step 3: Use E = Pt. Step 4: E = 1100 × 300 = 330000 J.
resources.marking_guidance: Credit unit conversion before substitution. Final answer may also be written as 330 kJ.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["A frequent mistake is to use minutes directly with watts. Watts are joules per second, so time must be in seconds."]
resources.teacher_notes: Model the order: convert, choose equation, substitute, state unit.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Calculation Drill — Waves
resources.student_prompt: Light travels through a clear plastic at 2.0 × 10^8 m/s. The speed of light in air is 3.0 × 10^8 m/s. Calculate the refractive index of the plastic.
resources.worked_solution: Use n = speed in air ÷ speed in material. n = (3.0 × 10^8) ÷ (2.0 × 10^8) = 1.5.
resources.marking_guidance: 1 mark for correct formula; 1 mark for substitution; 1 mark for n = 1.5 with no unit.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Refractive index has no unit because it is a ratio of two speeds."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use this to show that light slows down in optically denser media.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Worked Example — Waves
resources.student_prompt: Worked example: A ray enters a glass tile from air. The angle of incidence is 45° and the angle of refraction is 28°. Calculate the refractive index of the glass.
resources.worked_solution: Step 1: Use Snell's law for air to glass: n = sin i / sin r. Step 2: Substitute the angles: n = sin 45° / sin 28°. Step 3: Calculate the sine values: 0.707 / 0.469. Step 4: n = 1.51. The refractive index is about 1.5.
resources.marking_guidance: Credit correct sine ratio and final answer with no unit.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["The most common error is using the angles themselves instead of their sine values."]
resources.teacher_notes: Show students how to write calculator steps clearly.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Diagram Or Graph Drill — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: Sketch one complete cycle of the output voltage from a simple a.c. generator. Use a horizontal time axis and a vertical voltage axis. Label one peak positive voltage, one peak negative voltage, and the period.
resources.worked_solution: The graph should be a smooth sine-like wave crossing zero twice per cycle. It should have equal positive and negative peaks and one full cycle labelled from one matching point to the next.
resources.marking_guidance: Axes labelled time / s and voltage / V; smooth alternating curve; equal positive and negative peaks; period labelled correctly.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Some students draw a d.c. line above zero. A simple a.c. generator produces a voltage that changes direction."]
resources.teacher_notes: Good conceptual graph task before adding numerical scales.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Diagram Or Graph Drill — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: Draw a voltage-time graph for an a.c. generator with peak voltage 6.0 V and period 0.20 s. Show two complete cycles from t = 0 to t = 0.40 s.
resources.worked_solution: The graph should have peaks at +6.0 V and -6.0 V, repeat every 0.20 s, and show two cycles in 0.40 s. The curve should alternate symmetrically about 0 V.
resources.marking_guidance: Scale covers 0 to 0.40 s and at least -6 to +6 V; two complete cycles; correct amplitude; correct period; smooth curve.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["A common error is drawing only one cycle in 0.40 s, which would give a period of 0.40 s instead of 0.20 s."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use this to check amplitude and period separately.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Diagram Or Graph Drill — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: Two a.c. generator outputs are compared. Generator X has peak voltage 4 V and period 0.50 s. Generator Y rotates faster and has peak voltage 4 V and period 0.25 s. On the same axes, sketch both voltage-time graphs from 0 to 0.50 s and label X and Y.
resources.worked_solution: X should show one complete cycle in 0.50 s. Y should show two complete cycles in 0.50 s. Both should have the same peak values of +4 V and -4 V.
resources.marking_guidance: Axes labelled; X has one cycle; Y has two cycles; both have amplitude 4 V; both are centred on 0 V; labels clear.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students often increase the amplitude when the generator rotates faster. Faster rotation increases frequency; amplitude only changes if stated."]
resources.teacher_notes: Good task for distinguishing frequency from amplitude.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Marking Checklist — Electricity and magnetism
resources.worked_solution: Use this checklist to mark student a.c. generator output graphs: correct alternating shape; voltage changes sign; curve centred on zero; equal positive and negative peaks unless stated otherwise; correct period or frequency; correct amplitude; axes labelled with quantities and units; labels such as peak voltage and period placed accurately.
resources.marking_guidance: MP1 axes labelled time and voltage with units where numerical values are used; MP2 alternating output crosses zero; MP3 positive and negative peaks are shown; MP4 amplitude matches given value; MP5 period or number of cycles matches given value; MP6 smooth repeated waveform; MP7 comparison graphs are clearly labelled and not confused with d.c.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Teachers sometimes accept any wavy line. The key physics is that the output reverses direction and repeats periodically."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use as a reusable marking guide for generator graph sketches.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Graph Marking Checklist — Thermal physics
resources.worked_solution: Mark thermal-physics graph work for sensible scale, accurate plotting, and correct interpretation. Award credit for axes using the independent variable on the x-axis and the dependent variable on the y-axis, units in labels, plotted points within half a small square, a smooth curve or best-fit line as appropriate, and a valid reading from the graph.
resources.marking_guidance: MP1 independent variable on x-axis and dependent variable on y-axis; MP2 both axes labelled with units; MP3 scale uses at least half the grid and is linear; MP4 all points plotted accurately; MP5 best-fit curve or line follows the trend without dot-to-dot joins unless appropriate; MP6 anomalous point handled sensibly if present; MP7 gradient or interpolation read with units.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students may join every point with straight segments. For experimental thermal data, a smooth curve or best-fit trend is usually more appropriate."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use this checklist across cooling, heating and insulation investigations.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Graphing Drill — Thermal physics
resources.student_prompt: Cooling data are shown for a cup of warm water. Time / min: 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10. Temperature / °C: 72, 64, 58, 53, 49, 46. Plot temperature against time, draw a smooth best-fit curve, and estimate the temperature at 5 min.
resources.worked_solution: Put time on the x-axis and temperature on the y-axis. Plot the six points accurately. A smooth decreasing curve should pass close to the points. At 5 min, the temperature should be about 55 °C to 56 °C.
resources.marking_guidance: Axes with units; sensible scale; accurate points; smooth decreasing curve; interpolation at 5 min about 55-56 °C.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Some students plot temperature on the x-axis because it is listed second. The independent variable, time, goes on the x-axis."]
resources.teacher_notes: Good introductory thermal graph for interpolation.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Graphing Drill — Thermal physics
resources.student_prompt: An insulated container is tested. Time / min: 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15. Temperature / °C: 80, 73, 67, 62, 58, 55. Plot the graph and estimate the cooling rate between 3 min and 12 min.
resources.worked_solution: Plot temperature against time and draw a smooth curve. Cooling rate between 3 and 12 min can be estimated using the two data points: change in temperature = 58 - 73 = -15 °C over 9 min. Rate = -15/9 = -1.7 °C/min, so the temperature falls at about 1.7 °C/min.
resources.marking_guidance: Correct axes; accurate plotting; appropriate best-fit curve; working for gradient/rate; answer about 1.7 °C/min fall.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students may ignore the negative sign. In words, state that the temperature is decreasing."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use this to connect graph gradient to rate of cooling.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Graphing Drill — Thermal physics
resources.student_prompt: A heated metal block cools in air. Time / min: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Temperature / °C: 90, 78, 68, 60, 54, 50, 47. Plot the graph. Draw a tangent at t = 2 min and estimate the instantaneous cooling rate at that time.
resources.worked_solution: Plot a smooth decreasing curve. At t = 2 min, draw a tangent touching the curve. A reasonable tangent might pass near (1.0 min, 77 °C) and (3.5 min, 56 °C). Gradient = (56 - 77)/(3.5 - 1.0) = -21/2.5 = -8.4 °C/min. Accept a sensible estimate around 7 to 10 °C/min downward depending on the tangent.
resources.marking_guidance: Correct graph; smooth curve; tangent at 2 min; large triangle used for gradient; answer in °C/min with direction or negative sign.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Using two neighbouring data points gives an average rate, not an instantaneous rate from a tangent."]
resources.teacher_notes: Advanced practical graph skill; allow a range if tangent is sensible.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Graph Marking Checklist — Waves
resources.worked_solution: Mark wave-investigation graphs by checking that the chosen graph tests the relationship clearly, uses suitable scales, and supports a justified conclusion. Students should label axes with variables and units, plot all data accurately, draw a best-fit line or curve, calculate gradient where required, and use the graph to identify direct proportionality or non-linear behaviour.
resources.marking_guidance: MP1 correct variable on each axis; MP2 units included; MP3 linear scales and good use of grid; MP4 points plotted accurately; MP5 best-fit line or curve appropriate to trend; MP6 gradient calculated using a large triangle where required; MP7 conclusion refers to graph shape and data.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["A straight best-fit line is not always correct. The form of the expected relationship should guide line or curve choice."]
resources.teacher_notes: Reusable for wave speed, frequency, wavelength and oscillation investigations.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Graphing Drill — Waves
resources.student_prompt: Ripple-tank data are collected. Frequency / Hz: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. Wavelength / cm: 6.0, 3.0, 2.0, 1.5, 1.2. Plot wavelength against frequency and describe the trend.
resources.worked_solution: The graph should show wavelength decreasing as frequency increases. The trend is non-linear. This is consistent with a roughly constant wave speed because wavelength is inversely related to frequency.
resources.marking_guidance: Axes labelled frequency / Hz and wavelength / cm; accurate plotting; smooth decreasing curve; trend stated correctly.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students may say the relationship is directly proportional because both variables are wave quantities. The data show the opposite trend."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use this to discuss inverse relationships without heavy algebra.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Graphing Drill — Waves
resources.student_prompt: Data for water waves are shown. Frequency / Hz: 4, 6, 8, 10, 12. Wavelength / m: 0.50, 0.33, 0.25, 0.20, 0.17. Calculate the wave speed for each row, then plot wave speed against frequency.
resources.worked_solution: Use v = fλ. Speeds are 2.00, 1.98, 2.00, 2.00, 2.04 m/s. The graph of speed against frequency should be almost horizontal at about 2.0 m/s, showing nearly constant wave speed.
resources.marking_guidance: Correct speed calculations; axes labelled; points plotted near 2.0 m/s; best-fit line approximately horizontal; conclusion states speed is nearly constant.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["A common error is to plot wavelength again instead of calculated speed. The task asks for a processed variable."]
resources.teacher_notes: Good for introducing calculated columns in practical tables.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Graphing Drill — Waves
resources.student_prompt: A vibrating string is tested. Tension / N: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. Wave speed / m/s: 18, 25, 31, 36, 40. Plot wave speed against tension. Decide whether speed is directly proportional to tension.
resources.worked_solution: The graph increases but is not a straight line through the origin. If speed were directly proportional to tension, doubling tension from 10 N to 20 N would double speed from 18 m/s to 36 m/s. It only increases to 25 m/s. Therefore speed is not directly proportional to tension.
resources.marking_guidance: Accurate plot; suitable scale; smooth increasing curve; comparison with direct proportionality; justified conclusion using data.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students often think any increasing graph means direct proportionality. Direct proportionality requires a straight line through the origin."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use this for high-quality conclusion writing from graphs.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Experiment Planning Task — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: Plan an experiment to investigate how the length of a resistance wire affects the current in a circuit. You may use a low-voltage power supply, switch, ammeter, voltmeter, metre ruler, crocodile clips and resistance wire. Include the variables, method, table and graph.
resources.worked_solution: Set up a series circuit with the power supply, switch, ammeter and a measured length of resistance wire. Place a voltmeter across the wire and keep the supply voltage constant. Choose lengths such as 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 cm. For each length, close the switch briefly, record current and voltage, then open the switch to reduce heating. Repeat readings and calculate mean current. Independent variable: length of wire. Dependent variable: current. Control variables: wire material, wire diameter, supply voltage and temperature as far as possible. Table columns: length / cm, current 1 / A, current 2 / A, mean current / A, voltage / V. Plot mean current against length or resistance against length if resistance is calculated.
resources.marking_guidance: MP1 suitable circuit with ammeter in series; MP2 voltmeter across wire or voltage kept constant; MP3 length varied over at least five values; MP4 current measured for each length; MP5 controls stated; MP6 repeats and mean; MP7 suitable table with units; MP8 graph named.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students may put the ammeter in parallel. An ammeter must be in series to measure current through the wire."]
resources.teacher_notes: Keep currents low and switch off between readings to reduce heating effects.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Experiment Planning Task — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: Plan an experiment to investigate how the number of turns on a coil affects the strength of an electromagnet. You may use insulated copper wire, an iron nail, a low-voltage d.c. supply, switch, paper clips, ammeter and connecting leads. Include safety and reliability details.
resources.worked_solution: Wind insulated wire around an iron nail to make a coil. Connect the coil, switch, ammeter and low-voltage d.c. supply in series. Test numbers of turns such as 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50. For each trial, close the switch for the same short time and count the number of paper clips lifted. Open the switch after each trial to prevent heating. Keep the current as constant as possible by checking the ammeter and adjusting the supply if needed. Control variables include nail size/material, paper clip type, contact time, current and lifting method. Repeat each number of turns three times and calculate the mean number of clips. Table: turns, current / A, clips trial 1, clips trial 2, clips trial 3, mean clips. Plot mean number of clips against number of turns.
resources.marking_guidance: MP1 clear electromagnet setup; MP2 independent variable is number of turns; MP3 dependent variable measures strength; MP4 current controlled or monitored; MP5 other control variables; MP6 repeat readings; MP7 suitable table with units where needed; MP8 appropriate graph; MP9 safe low-voltage use and switch-off.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["A common weak plan changes both current and number of turns at the same time. Only one independent variable should be changed."]
resources.teacher_notes: Good extended planning item because students must operationalise 'strength'.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Planning Marking Checklist — Electricity and magnetism
resources.worked_solution: A strong practical plan should define a testable question, identify variables, describe apparatus setup, give a repeatable method, include controls, plan data recording, propose graphing/analysis and mention safety/reliability.
resources.marking_guidance: MP1 states a clear independent variable; MP2 states a measurable dependent variable; MP3 lists suitable apparatus and setup; MP4 gives a step-by-step method with at least five values of the independent variable; MP5 states key control variables; MP6 includes repeat readings and mean; MP7 gives a results table with headings and units; MP8 states an appropriate graph; MP9 includes safe low-voltage use, avoiding overheating, or other relevant safety detail; MP10 conclusion method links graph trend to the question.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Plans often lose marks because they are descriptive but not measurable. Every plan needs variables, measurements and a clear table."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use MP labels to train students to self-check before writing the final answer.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Data Interpretation Drill — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: An electrical component has the following data. Potential difference / V: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4. Current / A: 0, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60, 0.80. Describe the relationship between current and potential difference and calculate the resistance.
resources.worked_solution: The current increases in direct proportion to the potential difference because the ratio V/I is constant. Using V = 4 V and I = 0.80 A, R = V/I = 4/0.80 = 5.0 Ω.
resources.marking_guidance: Credit direct proportionality, constant resistance, correct calculation and unit ohm.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students may calculate I/V instead of V/I. Resistance is potential difference divided by current."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use as a simple ohmic conductor interpretation.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Data Interpretation Drill — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: A lamp is tested. Potential difference / V: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Current / A: 0, 0.18, 0.31, 0.41, 0.49, 0.55. Explain what happens to the resistance as potential difference increases.
resources.worked_solution: Calculate R = V/I at selected points. At 1 V, R = 1/0.18 = 5.6 Ω. At 5 V, R = 5/0.55 = 9.1 Ω. The resistance increases as potential difference increases. This is because the filament becomes hotter, making it harder for charge to flow.
resources.marking_guidance: Marks for calculating or comparing V/I, stating resistance increases, and giving heating explanation.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["A common error is to say resistance decreases because current still increases. Resistance depends on the ratio V/I, not just current."]
resources.teacher_notes: Good for identifying non-ohmic behaviour from data.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Data Interpretation Drill — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: Two components are tested. At 2.0 V, component A has current 0.40 A and component B has current 0.10 A. At 6.0 V, A has current 1.20 A and B has current 0.20 A. Identify which component is ohmic and justify your answer.
resources.worked_solution: For component A, V/I = 2.0/0.40 = 5.0 Ω and 6.0/1.20 = 5.0 Ω, so resistance is constant and A is ohmic. For component B, V/I = 2.0/0.10 = 20 Ω and 6.0/0.20 = 30 Ω, so resistance changes and B is non-ohmic.
resources.marking_guidance: Credit resistance calculations for both voltages, identification of A as ohmic, and justification using constant resistance.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Students may choose the component with smaller current as ohmic. Ohmic behaviour means constant resistance, not low current."]
resources.teacher_notes: Strong comparison item for evidence-based explanation.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Worked Example — Electricity and magnetism
resources.student_prompt: Worked example: A resistor is tested. Potential difference / V: 1.5, 3.0, 4.5, 6.0. Current / A: 0.30, 0.60, 0.90, 1.20. Decide whether the resistor is ohmic and find its resistance.
resources.worked_solution: Step 1: For an ohmic resistor, V is directly proportional to I and resistance is constant. Step 2: Calculate R = V/I using one pair: R = 3.0/0.60 = 5.0 Ω. Step 3: Check another pair: R = 6.0/1.20 = 5.0 Ω. Step 4: Since resistance is constant, the resistor is ohmic.
resources.marking_guidance: Award marks for two V/I checks, constant resistance conclusion, and correct unit.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["Only checking that current increases is not enough. Many non-ohmic components also have increasing current."]
resources.teacher_notes: Model how to justify a conclusion using more than one data point.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.

resources.title: Calculation Drill — Motion, forces and energy
resources.student_prompt: An electric shuttle travels in two parts: 12 m/s for 40 s, then 8.0 m/s for 55 s. Calculate the total distance travelled.
resources.worked_solution: Distance in first part = 12 × 40 = 480 m. Distance in second part = 8.0 × 55 = 440 m. Total distance = 480 + 440 = 920 m.
resources.marking_guidance: 1 mark for each section distance; 1 mark for adding the two distances; answer must include m.
resources.common_misconceptions: ["A common error is to average the speeds without considering the different times. Calculate each section separately."]
resources.teacher_notes: Use this to introduce piecewise motion before speed-time graph area.
resources.originality_statement: Original Quanta Aptus content generated from derived skill metadata only.
